summary: Jeff Atwood passionately defends netbooks against critics who dismiss them as inferior laptops, arguing they represent a pivotal democratizing force in computing. He contends that netbooks fulfill the promise of 'a PC on every desk' by providing affordable, unrestricted access to the internet and computing power without the gatekeepers and fees that plague smartphones. Using Dave Winer's definition of netbooks—emphasizing user freedom, competition, and openness—Atwood positions them as instruments of user empowerment rather than compromised devices. He demonstrates this with a review of the Acer Aspire 1410, showing that modern netbooks deliver remarkable specifications at under $500, making powerful computing accessible to everyone.
key_points: Netbooks represent the culmination of four decades of computing democratization, providing unrestricted internet access without gatekeepers or monthly fees
Smartphones will never be 'free PCs' because they remain locked behind corporate walled gardens and toll roads, while netbooks offer true user freedom
The pattern of incumbent tech dismissing cheaper alternatives repeats throughout history (mainframes vs PCs, laptops vs netbooks)—and users always win
Modern CULV-based netbooks like the Acer Aspire 1410 offer remarkable performance (dual-core, 5-hour battery, 1366x768 screen) at just $439
Intel deliberately hobbles Atom CPUs to prevent netbook price erosion and protect market segmentation
money_quotes: Netbooks are the endpoint of four decades of computing – the final, ubiquitous manifestation of 'A PC on every desk and in every home.'
I'm talking about unlimited access to the complete sum of human knowledge, and free, unfettered communication with anyone on earth. For everyone.
I don't care how 'smart' your smartphone is, it will never escape those corporate shackles.
Smartphones are simply not free enough to deliver the type of democratic transformation that netbooks absolutely will.
That's why I love netbooks. In all their cheap, crappy glory. And you should too. Because they're instruments of user power.
The truly significant thing is this – the users took over.
Netbooks aren't an alternative to notebook computers. They are the new computers.

They may be pieces of junk to Mr. Jobs, but to me, these modest little boxes are marvels – inspiring evidence of the inexorable march of powerful, open computing technology to everyman and everywhere.
key_insight: The true value of netbooks lies not in their specifications but in their role as instruments of democratic access to computing—cheap, open, and free from corporate gatekeepers.
